Golda Meir- Iron Lady of Israel

# Golda Meir- Iron Lady of Israel

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Mar 17, 2026

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

3

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

1

Share

Golda Meir, was the first and only woman Prime Minister of Israel, often described as the “strong-willed, straight-talking, grey-bunned grandmother of the Jewish people”. One of the finest woman political leaders in history.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!q9M3!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F48f682c9-763d-4115-965f-04058ee43176_602x712.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!q9M3!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F48f682c9-763d-4115-965f-04058ee43176_602x712.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

She was from Ukraine(then part of Russia), born in the capital city of Kyiv, her original surname was Mabovitch. Her father Moshe was a carpenter, she had two siblings Sheyna, Tzipke. n her autobiography she recalls how her father used to board up the front door of their home, in response to rumors of a pogrom.

Her father later migrated to US, where he settled down in Milwaukee, and bought his family along. She later moved to Denver, where her sister Sheyna had settled with her husband. After the hardships of Kyiv and the immigrant struggles in Milwaukee, Denver opened her world to ideas. Living with her sister Sheyna and brother-in-law, she found herself in a household where debates about Zionism, socialism, women’s rights, and literature were constant. She later described it as the place where her “real education” began.

*It is not only a matter, I believe, of religious observance and practice. To me, being Jewish means and has always meant being proud to be part of a people that has maintained its distinct identity for more than 2,000 years.*

Her attraction to **Socialist Zionism** gave her both a political and moral compass, and her marriage to Morris Meyerson in 1917 was bound up with a condition: they would settle in Palestine. By 1921, she, Morris, and her sister Sheyna made that leap, joining a kibbutz.

The kibbutz years were formative. They embodied the socialist ideals she admired,communal living, shared labor, collective responsibility. Though she was personally an atheist, she identified deeply with Judaism as a cultural and national identity, which gave her a sense of belonging and purpose.

*“I believe in the Jewish people, and the Jewish people believe in God”* was quintessential Golda Meir, pragmatic, layered, and deeply rooted in identity rather than doctrine. It captures her paradox perfectly. Personally an atheist, yet she saw Judaism not only as a religion but as a collective destiny, a people bound by history, struggle, and faith.

This statement also reflects her political genius. By framing her belief in the Jewish people as inseparable from their belief in God, she positioned herself as both secular and faithful, bridging divides within Israeli society.

The 1938 **Évian Conference**, where representatives of 32 countries expressed sympathy for Jewish refugees but refused to accept them, was pivotal in shaping Golda Meir’s worldview. For her, it was proof that the world’s compassion was hollow without action. The rejection of fleeing Jews by most nations reinforced her conviction that only a sovereign Jewish homeland could guarantee safety and dignity.

Her words capture the essence of that realization: *“There is only one thing I hope to see before I die and that is that my people should not need expressions of sympathy anymore.”*

In 1948, as Israel fought for survival against the Arab states, she toured the United States to raise funds. When told she could not possibly raise more than \$8 million, she took it as a challenge, and returned with \$50 million, traveling tirelessly across America. David Ben-Gurion’s praise, calling her the “Jewish woman who got the money which made the state possible”, was not hyperbole, it was recognition that her determination literally financed Israel’s survival.

Her daring was not limited to fundraising. Disguised as an Arab woman, she crossed into Jordan to meet King Abdullah I, urging him not to join the war. When he asked why she was in such a hurry, her reply was legendary: *“We have been waiting for 2000 years.”*

And then, the emotional climax:, signing Israel’s Declaration of Independence. She later recalled, *“After I signed, I cried. When I studied American history as a schoolgirl and I read about those who signed the U.S. Declaration of Independence, I couldn’t imagine these were real people doing something real. And there I was sitting down and signing a declaration.”* That moment fused her immigrant childhood in Milwaukee with the destiny of a new nation.

As **Labour Minister**, she was instrumental in shaping Israel’s welfare state. She oversaw the integration of waves of immigrants into the workforce, and pushed for the construction of schools, hospitals, roads, and housing blocks.

When she became **Foreign Minister in 1956**, she adopted the surname *Meir* (meaning “illuminate”) on Moshe Sharett’s advice, to give her name a Hebrew resonance. In that role, she expanded Israel’s presence abroad, especially in Africa. She undertook a series of visits to newly independent African nations, offering support in their struggles against colonial powers.

The Munich Massacre of 1972, when 11 Israeli athletes were murdered during the Olympics, was a turning point. At first, she appealed to the world to take action against the perpetrators. But when global leaders turned away, refusing to confront the killers, she made a daring and controversial decision: she authorized Mossad to track down and assassinate those responsible, members of Black September and the PFLP.

It was a stark example of her **straightforward, no-nonsense approach**. For Meir, justice and security for her people outweighed diplomatic hesitation. She understood that sympathy without action was meaningless,echoing the conviction she had carried since the Évian Conference decades earlier.

In the 1970s, when nearly **200,000 Soviet Jews** were emigrating to Israel via Austria, Palestinian militants seized seven emigrants as hostages. In response, Austrian Chancellor Bruno Kreisky closed down the transit facility, effectively cutting off the lifeline for Soviet Jews.

Golda Meir refused to accept this. She exerted intense pressure on Austria, forcing Kreisky to reopen a new transit camp. For her, this wasn’t just about seven hostages, it was about the principle that Jewish emigration could not be held hostage to terrorism. She saw it as a test of Israel’s responsibility to protect Jews everywhere, not just within its borders.

As Prime Minister during the Yom Kippur War of 1973, she faced Israel’s gravest existential crisis. Though Israel ultimately won, Meir was accused of poor intelligence and lack of preparedness. The shock of the initial Arab attack left scars on the nation, and her leadership,though resolute,was scrutinized. She won the elections later that year, but the weight of criticism and her own sense of responsibility led her to resign in 1974, saying she had served enough time.

This resignation was not a retreat but a recognition of limits. It showed her integrity, she would not cling to power for its own sake.

Sources   
https://www.amazon.in/My-Life-by-Golda-Meir/dp/0399116699
